intro: Running training is built on physiological principles (adaptation, supercompensation, sport-specificity). Intensity zones, weekly training load, and recovery are pillars of performance. Predict your half marathon time from your 10K result—free and instant. Our data is current through 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you informed.

fields.label: 10K Time (minutes)
fields.help: Your fastest 10K race time in minutes.

explanation:
**Running training** combines science (physiology, biomechanics) and practice. Intensity zones, progressive load, rest, and nutrition form the complete system.

## How It Works

Predict your half marathon time from your 10K result using proven race prediction formulas. This calculator applies the formula with your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting you to values outside normal expectations.

## Important Notes

This calculation is an estimate for guidance only. For critical decisions (health, competition planning, medical), consult with a specialized professional. Our data is current through 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: How accurate is this half marathon projection?
faq.a: The projection is based on scientifically validated formulas. Actual performance varies with factors like temperature, altitude, fitness level, and race day conditions.

faq.q: What's the difference between Cameron and Riegel formulas?
faq.a: Cameron's formula is optimized for middle and long distances (half marathon and marathon), while Riegel's is more general across all distances.

faq.q: Why might my actual race time differ from the prediction?
faq.a: Many factors affect race performance: weather, course elevation, training consistency, sleep, nutrition, and mental readiness on race day.

faq.q: What assumptions does this calculator make?
faq.a: The calculator assumes consistent training, adequate rest, good recovery practices, and similar race conditions to your 10K performance.

faq.q: How can I improve my projected half marathon time?
faq.a: Focus on: consistent training, progressive mileage, long runs, speed work (intervals), adequate recovery, and proper nutrition.

faq.q: Is this calculator free to use?
faq.a: Yes, all our calculators are completely free with no registration required.

faq.q: What is VDOT and how does it relate to race prediction?
faq.a: VDOT (VO₂ max proxy) is a training metric developed by running coach Jack Daniels. It helps calculate recommended paces for different training zones from one recent race time.

faq.q: Do you store my data or results?
faq.a: No. All calculations happen in your browser. We don't send any data to our servers or track your results.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, does not require external data updates
